---
title: ""
page-layout: full
---

```{=html}
<div class="proj-page">

  <!-- PAGE HEADER -->
  <header class="proj-header">
    <div class="proj-header-inner">
      <div class="proj-back">
        <a href="../../index.html" class="proj-back-link">← All Projects</a>
      </div>
      <p class="proj-eyebrow">Visual Analytics · Network Analysis · AML Typologies</p>
      <h1 class="proj-title">Art Markets &amp; the PEP Problem</h1>
      <p class="proj-subtitle">
        The art market's opacity makes it a documented vehicle for laundering —
        especially among politically exposed persons. This project combines auction
        data, ICIJ offshore leaks, and PEP network analysis to visualise how money
        and influence move through high-value cultural assets.
      </p>
      <div class="proj-header-rule"></div>
      <div class="proj-tag-row">
        <span class="proj-tag">R</span>
        <span class="proj-tag">Tableau</span>
        <span class="proj-tag">ICIJ Offshore Leaks</span>
        <span class="proj-tag">Network Analysis</span>
        <span class="proj-tag">FATF Typologies</span>
        <span class="proj-tag proj-tag--status">In Progress ◎</span>
      </div>
    </div>
  </header>

  <!-- BODY -->
  <div class="proj-body">

    <!-- Section 1 -->
    <div class="proj-section">
      <div class="proj-section-marker">
        <span class="proj-section-num">I</span>
        <div class="proj-section-line"></div>
      </div>
      <div class="proj-section-content">
        <h2 class="proj-section-title">Why the art market</h2>
        <p class="proj-prose">
          Art is unusual as an asset class: it is portable, subjective in value,
          and largely unregulated compared to financial instruments of equivalent worth.
          The FATF's 2023 guidance on art market money laundering is explicit — auction
          houses, galleries, and freeport storage facilities represent a documented
          typology vector, particularly for PEPs seeking to move and obscure wealth
          across jurisdictions without triggering standard financial monitoring.
        </p>
        <p class="proj-prose">
          The connection between art markets and PEPs is not theoretical. The Panama
          and Pandora Papers contain documented examples of offshore structures used
          to purchase high-value art, with ownership deliberately obscured through
          shell companies and nominee arrangements. This project is an attempt to
          surface that pattern analytically.
        </p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Section 2 -->
    <div class="proj-section">
      <div class="proj-section-marker">
        <span class="proj-section-num">II</span>
        <div class="proj-section-line"></div>
      </div>
      <div class="proj-section-content">
        <h2 class="proj-section-title">Method &amp; approach</h2>
        <p class="proj-prose">
          The project works across three linked analytical threads: price anomaly
          detection in public auction records, geographic mapping of freeport flows
          (Geneva, Singapore, Luxembourg, Delaware), and cross-referencing against
          ICIJ offshore entity data and the OpenSanctions PEP database to identify
          counterparty overlap.
        </p>
        <p class="proj-prose">
          Where a named auction buyer or seller appears in both public art market
          records and ICIJ offshore data — or maps to a known PEP entity — that
          intersection becomes the unit of analysis. The output is not a legal
          accusation; it's a demonstration of what the data makes visible when
          you connect sources that are rarely put side by side.
        </p>

        <!-- Method rows -->
        <div class="proj-sources">
          <p class="proj-sources-label">Data Sources</p>
          <div class="proj-source-row">
            <div class="proj-source-index">01</div>
            <div class="proj-source-body">
              <p class="proj-source-name">ICIJ Offshore Leaks Database</p>
              <p class="proj-source-desc">Panama Papers, Pandora Papers — offshore entity structures, beneficial ownership networks, and jurisdiction mapping.</p>
            </div>
          </div>
          <div class="proj-source-row">
            <div class="proj-source-index">02</div>
            <div class="proj-source-body">
              <p class="proj-source-name">Public Auction Records</p>
              <p class="proj-source-desc">Christie's and Sotheby's publicly available sale results — lot prices, sale dates, buyer/seller regions for anomaly analysis.</p>
            </div>
          </div>
          <div class="proj-source-row">
            <div class="proj-source-index">03</div>
            <div class="proj-source-body">
              <p class="proj-source-name">OpenSanctions PEP Database</p>
              <p class="proj-source-desc">Structured dataset of politically exposed persons across jurisdictions — used for counterparty cross-referencing and network construction.</p>
            </div>
          </div>
          <div class="proj-source-row">
            <div class="proj-source-index">04</div>
            <div class="proj-source-body">
              <p class="proj-source-name">Basel Art Market Report</p>
              <p class="proj-source-desc">Annual market sizing and geographic flow data — contextual baseline for understanding scale and opacity of the global art trade.</p>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Section 3 -->
    <div class="proj-section">
      <div class="proj-section-marker">
        <span class="proj-section-num">III</span>
        <div class="proj-section-line"></div>
      </div>
      <div class="proj-section-content">
        <h2 class="proj-section-title">Expected output</h2>
        <p class="proj-prose">
          An interactive Tableau dashboard visualising auction price anomalies and
          geographic freeport flows alongside an R network visualisation of PEP
          counterparty connections. The written analysis maps findings to FATF's
          2023 art market ML typology guidance — grounding the visual work in
          regulatory vocabulary relevant to CDD and EDD practice.
        </p>
        <p class="proj-prose">
          The goal is not to reinvent forensic accounting. It is to show what becomes
          visible when you combine public data sources with AML typology thinking —
          and to practice the kind of cross-source analysis that underlies real
          financial crime intelligence work.
        </p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Living notice -->
    <div class="proj-notice">
      <span class="proj-notice-mark">✦</span>
      <p class="proj-notice-text">
        <em>Work in progress — visualisations and full analysis will be published
        here as the project develops.</em>
      </p>
    </div>

  </div>

</div>
```
